title: practical english usage - Part 55
content:
For more about the way information is organised in sentences, ►    267.

For -ing forms with passive meanings after need and want (e.g. My watch needs cleaning), ►     100..1.

For more about reflexive verbs, ►     178.   For active and passive past participles, ►    96.3-96.4.

She opened the door / The door opened (►  9).

And some active verbs can have both active and passive meanings, for example for example There’s a lot of work to do / to be done (for details, ►     106).

Sometimes active and passive infinitives can be used with very similar meanings: for example to worry / to be worried; to drown / to be drowned (►  438).

Some verbs can be used in both active and passive forms with similar meanings: grammar •  57  passive structures and verb forms not They have a nice house. (Birr       A-nice-house is had-by-them:) resemble, suit.

(verbs which  refer to states, not actions). Examples are fit, have, lack, verbs, too, are seldom used in the passive. Most of these are 'stative verbs’ there is nothing to become the subject of a passive sentence. Some transitive intransitive verbs (►  7.2) like die or arrive, which  cannot have objects, because Not all verbs can have passive forms. Passive structures are impossible with 3 verbs     not   used    in  the   passive Alex has been arrested! The police have arrested Alex!

talk about finished actions with present consequences (►   47).

And the present perfect passive can be used, like the present perfect active, to The secretary is preparing the papers now.

The papers are being prepared now.

active, to talk about things that are going on at the time of speaking (►  32).

example the present progressive passive is used, like the present progressive Passive tenses are normally used in the same way as active tenses. So for 2 use   of  tenses For more about structures with prepositions at the ends of clauses, ►    209.

He hates being made a fool of.

She likes being looked at. I need to be taken care of.

The furniture was broken up for firewood.

forms if they are transitive.

Note that verbs made up of more than one word (►   12-13)  can have passive Examples of passive -ing forms: being watched; having been invited.

Examples of passive infinitives: (to) be taken; (to) have been invited.

(has been being + pp) are unusual.

Future progressive passives (will be being + pp) and perfect progressive passives past perfect          had been + pp             I knew why I had been chosen.

past progressive          was/were being + pp     I felt as if I was being watched.

simple past                 was! were + pp             I wasn't invited, but I went.

by Tuesday.

future perfect       will have been + pp     Everything will have been done (simple) future           will be + pp                   You'll be told soon enough.

present perfect           have/has been + pp      Has Emily been told?

is being painted.

followed by the past participle (= pp) of the verb. (For get as a passive auxiliary, We   normally make passive forms of a verb by using tenses of the auxiliary be 1 passive      verb    forms 57 passive      structu     res   and   verb    forms grammar •  58  by + agent He was shot (by the policeman) with a rifle.

an action (►  416).

With is used when we  talk about an instrument which  is used by an agent to do Are you frightened of spiders?

We were worried about/by her silence.

I was shocked at/by your attitude.

of by. (And such structures are not really passives, but examples of be + shocked, worried, frightened. After these, we  often use other prepositions instead Some past participles can be more like adjectives than verbs (►  96): for example These carpets are made by children who work twelve hours a day.
